# Transcrição: The Tracking Trinity Explained

- **Fonte:** The Tracking Trinity Explained
- **Duração:** 08:36
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

## [00:00 - 03:00]

So I think I may have got your curiosity. I hope I have on what exactly is the tracking Trinity. So as a reminder, I talk about the attribution trinity and also the tracking trinity. This is it. This is it in one pager. This is the simplest format I could get it into to explain to you why this is so important. The tracking trinity is made up of three things. The browser pixel, which you should know what that is, hopefully the server pixel. I've called it the server pixel just to explain the difference, which is CAPI conversion API and also the raw sales data from your sales platform, which is offline conversions. As you can see from the arrows on the right here, two of those are fed directly from your sales platform. So one of the arguments I see is, well, we don't need offline conversions because we've got CAPI. Well, guess what? There are other benefits of using offline conversions. Yes, that data is sent through CAPI and yes, there could be some duplication, but as I'll explain in later videos, there are a number of other benefits of having offline conversions as well. Now let's look at the left-hand side and see what's actually being sent to Facebook. So with a browser pixel, following the changes made that were enforced from Apple and the platform changes, the data changes that Facebook made, the first change they made was to limit us in the reported data that we can see and the way we optimize our assets to seven days. So prior to that, you could do 28 day. And what Facebook have done with that is, it means if you have sales that occur outside of seven days, they won't get tracked to your ads. That's a problem in itself. And it also means that your optimizations, especially if you have people that buy over a period of time that's longer than seven days, that's also a problem as well. And then on top of that, the data loss is high. We are seeing ad accounts reporting 30%, 40%. In some cases, 70% fewer conversions in ads manager versus what we're seeing in the backend. Now how do we know this? Because a lot of the clients we work with, our own ad campaigns right now are mostly running ads into Facebook. So knowing that and seeing that sales are, attribute in the backend that we can track in Google Analytics, we can see that Facebook is under serving. So the problem with this is if you, there's twofold, there's you as the advertiser, and then there's the machine, which is the Facebook algorithm. We both need data to make decisions. If Facebook is not getting that data, it can't optimize as well as it maybe could have a year ago, two years ago. If you don't have that data to see as well, because bear in mind there is some delayed attribution and things like that, as well as data loss, then that kind of creates a problem as well,

## [03:00 - 06:03]

which I hope you realize by now. So the browser pixel is a problem, but we still need it. In order to counter the loss of data, especially from the point of view of people having cookies disabled and things like that, Facebook developed the conversion API as a way of bypassing any restrictions on your browser. So where that really kicks in is when you install the conversion API, effectively it's the server-based pixel. It's not as simple as that, but I think for simplicity, that's the easiest way to understand this. Browser pixel, which is Chrome or Safari-based pixel, this is from your server. So your sales platform, it could be Shopify, it could be Magento, it could be WooCommerce, it could be ClickFunnels, whatever it is, that will push that data through Cappy into Facebook. So that reduces your data loss, but it's still not solving some of the major problems because of the seven-day click. We have one client, for example, we know that it takes up to 14, maybe 20 days for that customer to convert. And we know for a fact, having run their ad account since 2019, that when the iOS 14 changes kicked in, we saw that loss of data. So it's obvious, we know that. What that means is the data we see on Facebook for that account, for example, doesn't show all of that data. However, using offline conversions, we can see a lot more data. So what Facebook are doing here is, and this may change in the future, when you upload offline conversions, Facebook are not necessarily applying the changes enforced by Apple. Why is that? It's because Facebook made the changes in reaction to browser-based tracking. That was really the crux of the problem that Apple raised is of advertisers and ad platforms chasing people around the internet and retargeting them and things like that. So the agreement has been that Facebook will not track people on iOS 14. And this is where a lot of the data loss happens, or iOS 14.5 and onwards to be accurate, if they've opted out of tracking. And we know that a lot of people have opted out of tracking. So therefore the browser pixel loses a lot of data. The data is still being sent to Facebook, but they're not allowed to use it for reporting, for optimization and anything like that. So you may have seen, for example, if you're going to events manager, you'll see at times, you know, add to cart pixel and other pixel events are getting traffic, but then you look at ads manager and say, well, why am I not seeing my ads report? Probably and possibly because of the iOS 14 block. So how do we, one of the ways that we overcome that

## [06:03 - 08:36]

is to use offline conversions. And as I'll show you in a separate video, when you upload those offline conversions, you get data which is otherwise blocked when you use either of the pixel based methods, browser pixel or server pixel. And that's one of the benefits of using offline conversions. The other benefit is you can also create audiences from your offline conversions, which may include people that have been not tracked via the pixel. I'll just put it that way. I won't go into the full detail, but that's one of the things you can do. So you can create custom audiences from them. You can create look-alike audiences from them as well. And you can also right now, and I hope that Facebook do keep this, you can create breakdown data reports based on offline conversions. And again, I'll show you this in the reporting. So for example, if your offline conversion data contains gender, age, country, county, that data can be surfaced in ads manager. The same way we used to be able to do before these changes occurred. So that's one of the many benefits of using offline conversions. Now, unfortunately, because of some of the overlap between browser, so I'll just say the pixel based events and offline, you can't necessarily use the kind of some of those numbers completely and say, right, this is my exact ROAS. However, for decision making, we do use a combination of that data. And again, I'll show you how to use that. For some of you, if you have people that are buying within one day, you might say, well, people are within the seven day click window, I'm okay doing pixel based tracking browser and server and I'm fine. But the problem again is you're probably missing out on conversions that are happening outside of iOS 14. And also bear in mind as browsers start to kicking more and more privacy, this browser pixel is gonna suffer. It will all be relying a lot more on the server pixel. But if there are still changes that Facebook make and optimizations and things like that, you still wanna push those offline conversions through. So this part of the fat framework is really about making sure that the ad platform has enough data to optimize and be able to target and report. And then you have enough data so that you can make the right decisions on what's actually working and what's not.
